# IslandPilot: Hierarchical Regime Discovery and Per-Regime Evolution for Grid-Hedged Martingale Strategies

**Abstract**

Grid-hedged Martingale strategies on liquid foreign exchange markets such as EUR-USD present a structural challenge. Compounding spread, swap, and slippage costs erode the directional edge required for positive expectancy, while geometric depth schedules can convert a sequence of small adverse moves into catastrophic drawdown. This research asks whether regime-aware evolutionary optimisation can render such a strategy survivable under realistic execution costs, and isolates the structural limits of the approach. It introduces IslandPilot, a pipeline that couples hierarchical market regime discovery with an island-model genetic algorithm in which each discovered regime maintains an isolated genetic population evolving independently. The principal architectural contribution is that the island topology is derived from the regime structure itself. A two-level Gaussian Mixture Model with Bayesian Information Criterion selection partitions a 30-indicator feature space into macro-clusters and sub-clusters. Populations sharing a macro-cluster exchange genomes via ring migration, while populations in distinct macro-clusters evolve without interaction. Unlike prior island-model work in which topology is specified independently of the problem domain, the migration graph here is derived from the clustering hierarchy itself. Each island evolves the complete strategy parameter space (entry signal type, directional bias, grid geometry, sizing curve, and risk management controls), producing regime-specific execution policies. All fitness evaluations are conducted on the full production execution engine with real per-candle OANDA bid-ask spread, swap, and slippage costs; this research shows empirically that parameters evolved on simplified, cost-free simulators fail to transfer to live execution conditions. Applied to a grid-hedged Martingale strategy on EUR-USD at 5-minute resolution, the system trains on 36 months of data (2022 to 2024) through approximately 12,600 real-engine fitness evaluations (wall-clock approximately 10 hours 33 minutes on parallel cloud compute), discovering 63 regime leaves across 10 macro-clusters. On a 15.5-month strictly out-of-sample evaluation (January 2025 to mid-April 2026) under identical cost conditions, the pipeline reduces net loss from -87.4% (baseline) to -0.8% (an 86.6 percentage-point improvement), reduces maximum drawdown from 84.7% to 0.75% (roughly a 113-fold reduction), and lowers peak equity usage from 63.7% to 10.3%, while raising profit factor from 0.72 to 0.88. The mechanism is regime-conditioned depth capping and selectivity rather than improved entry quality: the pipeline opens 72 sessions against the baseline's 1,619, and its Level-0 win rate of 5.6% is in fact lower than the baseline's 26.4%. The honest reading is that regime-structured evolutionary optimisation cannot manufacture directional alpha that is absent from a random-entry Martingale on a mean-zero spot FX signal, but it bounds risk aggressively enough to convert a catastrophic strategy into a near-breakeven one under realistic execution costs. This research reports both the conditions under which the approach succeeds (capital preservation, drawdown bounding) and where it does not (alpha generation), contributing an honest accounting of a regime-structured evolutionary method's structural limits.

**Keywords:** island-model genetic algorithm; market regime detection; Gaussian mixture model; grid-hedged Martingale; evolutionary parameter optimisation; hierarchical clustering; adaptive position sizing; capital preservation; out-of-sample validation
